name: Frontend Platform and Quality — additive questions
description: Question expansion derived from the attached source files; reuses existing repository topic IDs and defines only missing concepts.

questions.title: Applying DOM event propagation and delegation in practice
questions.prompt: A list container listens for clicks and uses closest to identify the clicked item, including items added later. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains propagation phases
expectedPoints.description: Orders capture, target, and bubble correctly.

expectedPoints.label: Explains delegation
expectedPoints.description: Uses an ancestor plus target or closest for descendants.

expectedPoints.label: Separates control methods
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes preventDefault from stopPropagation.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about DOM event propagation and delegation
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Checking only event.target when a nested child may be clicked." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains propagation phases
expectedPoints.description: Orders capture, target, and bubble correctly.

expectedPoints.label: Explains delegation
expectedPoints.description: Uses an ancestor plus target or closest for descendants.

expectedPoints.label: Separates control methods
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes preventDefault from stopPropagation.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Stopping propagation globally to work around one component bug?

questions.title: Applying Responsive CSS Sizing and Layout in practice
questions.prompt: border-box keeps a declared card width inclusive of padding and border; min-width: 0 may let a flex child shrink. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the box model
expectedPoints.description: Correctly contrasts content-box and border-box.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses a layout model
expectedPoints.description: Relates Flexbox and Grid to one- and two-dimensional needs.

expectedPoints.label: Handles intrinsic responsiveness
expectedPoints.description: Mentions fluid sizing, min-content, overflow, or container queries.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Responsive CSS Sizing and Layout
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Adding more breakpoints instead of fixing intrinsic overflow." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the box model
expectedPoints.description: Correctly contrasts content-box and border-box.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses a layout model
expectedPoints.description: Relates Flexbox and Grid to one- and two-dimensional needs.

expectedPoints.label: Handles intrinsic responsiveness
expectedPoints.description: Mentions fluid sizing, min-content, overflow, or container queries.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Assuming a flex child always shrinks below its min-content size?

questions.title: Applying Web accessibility fundamentals in practice
questions.prompt: A button element already supplies keyboard activation and role, while a clickable div requires fragile reimplementation. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Starts with semantics
expectedPoints.description: Prefers native elements and correct roles.

expectedPoints.label: Covers interaction
expectedPoints.description: Checks keyboard operation, focus order, and visible focus.

expectedPoints.label: Checks naming, state, and tests
expectedPoints.description: Mentions labels, announced state, automation, and manual assistive testing.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Web accessibility fundamentals
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Using ARIA to repair a non-semantic element when a native element exists." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Starts with semantics
expectedPoints.description: Prefers native elements and correct roles.

expectedPoints.label: Covers interaction
expectedPoints.description: Checks keyboard operation, focus order, and visible focus.

expectedPoints.label: Checks naming, state, and tests
expectedPoints.description: Mentions labels, announced state, automation, and manual assistive testing.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Removing focus outlines without an equally visible replacement?

questions.title: Applying Core Web Vitals and frontend performance in practice
questions.prompt: A slow LCP image may need priority and correct sizing; poor INP may require splitting long main-thread tasks. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Defines all three metrics
expectedPoints.description: Correctly links loading, responsiveness, and stability.

expectedPoints.label: Connects causes to metrics
expectedPoints.description: Mentions resources or server for LCP, long tasks for INP, and reserved space for CLS.

expectedPoints.label: Uses evidence
expectedPoints.description: Combines profiling or traces with field and lab data.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Core Web Vitals and frontend performance
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Treating all performance problems as bundle-size problems." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Defines all three metrics
expectedPoints.description: Correctly links loading, responsiveness, and stability.

expectedPoints.label: Connects causes to metrics
expectedPoints.description: Mentions resources or server for LCP, long tasks for INP, and reserved space for CLS.

expectedPoints.label: Uses evidence
expectedPoints.description: Combines profiling or traces with field and lab data.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Optimizing a synthetic score without comparing field metrics and user journeys?

questions.title: Applying Browser XSS Defenses in practice
questions.prompt: Start at the output sink and choose an API that matches the intended content. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Prevents injection at the sink
expectedPoints.description: Uses safe rendering and context-aware encoding or sanitization.

expectedPoints.label: Explains CSP as defense in depth
expectedPoints.description: States what CSP restricts and that it is not a replacement for safe code.

expectedPoints.label: Treats HTML as untrusted
expectedPoints.description: Explains that third-party or user-authored HTML needs explicit sanitization and a narrow allowlist before reaching an HTML sink.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Browser XSS Defenses
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Treating a regular expression as a complete HTML sanitizer." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Prevents injection at the sink
expectedPoints.description: Uses safe rendering and context-aware encoding or sanitization.

expectedPoints.label: Explains CSP as defense in depth
expectedPoints.description: States what CSP restricts and that it is not a replacement for safe code.

expectedPoints.label: Treats HTML as untrusted
expectedPoints.description: Explains that third-party or user-authored HTML needs explicit sanitization and a narrow allowlist before reaching an HTML sink.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Assuming React escaping or CSP makes every third-party HTML value safe?

questions.title: Applying Same-origin policy, CORS, and browser credentials in practice
questions.prompt: A credentialed frontend request needs an explicit allowed origin and credentials permission; wildcard origin is not valid for that case. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Defines origin isolation
expectedPoints.description: Explains same-origin policy and the origin tuple.

expectedPoints.label: Explains CORS flow
expectedPoints.description: Covers response headers, preflight, and credentials rules.

expectedPoints.label: Sets the correct boundary
expectedPoints.description: States that CORS is browser enforcement, not API authentication.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Same-origin policy, CORS, and browser credentials
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Using Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * together with credentials." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Defines origin isolation
expectedPoints.description: Explains same-origin policy and the origin tuple.

expectedPoints.label: Explains CORS flow
expectedPoints.description: Covers response headers, preflight, and credentials rules.

expectedPoints.label: Sets the correct boundary
expectedPoints.description: States that CORS is browser enforcement, not API authentication.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Treating a CORS failure as proof that the API endpoint is protected from non-browser access?

questions.title: Applying Microfrontend architecture in practice
questions.prompt: Domain teams deploy separate page regions through module federation while sharing a versioned design system and platform contract. Review the design. Which assumptions and trade-offs matter, and how would you validate it at scale?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies real drivers
expectedPoints.description: Connects the pattern to team autonomy and independent delivery.

expectedPoints.label: Explains integration choices
expectedPoints.description: Mentions composition approach and explicit contracts.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes systemic costs
expectedPoints.description: Covers UX consistency, duplication, auth, observability, or failure isolation.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Microfrontend architecture
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Adopting microfrontends for a small team without independent deployment pressure." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies real drivers
expectedPoints.description: Connects the pattern to team autonomy and independent delivery.

expectedPoints.label: Explains integration choices
expectedPoints.description: Mentions composition approach and explicit contracts.

expectedPoints.label: Recognizes systemic costs
expectedPoints.description: Covers UX consistency, duplication, auth, observability, or failure isolation.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Sharing one mutable global runtime that removes the intended autonomy?

questions.title: Applying New-Window Navigation Isolation in practice
questions.prompt: Treat every external navigation as a trust-boundary decision. What would you validate or change before approving it, and how would you test the protections?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains opener isolation
expectedPoints.description: Connects noopener to removing the opened page's usable window.opener reference and preventing reverse tabnabbing.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes noreferrer
expectedPoints.description: Explains that noreferrer suppresses referrer information and is a separate privacy or compatibility decision.

expectedPoints.label: Treats navigation as a trust boundary
expectedPoints.description: Applies isolation consistently to untrusted destinations, redirects, and shared link components.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about New-Window Navigation Isolation
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Assuming target="_blank" is only a presentation choice." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains opener isolation
expectedPoints.description: Connects noopener to removing the opened page's usable window.opener reference and preventing reverse tabnabbing.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes noreferrer
expectedPoints.description: Explains that noreferrer suppresses referrer information and is a separate privacy or compatibility decision.

expectedPoints.label: Treats navigation as a trust boundary
expectedPoints.description: Applies isolation consistently to untrusted destinations, redirects, and shared link components.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using noreferrer everywhere without considering analytics or legitimate referrer needs?
